_provenance.extracted_by: in-session Claude Code sub-agent (no API call), 2026-06-22
_provenance.purpose: Real-content case study: does the novelty detector recognize that problem_07 Q1 is structurally an instance of the Banach Fixed-Point Theorem (known machinery on a relabeled map)? BASE = abstract Banach theorem; TARGET = problem_07 Q1 (Banach applied to a retraining map T). Shared functor vocabulary lets SME align them; every symbol is grounded to a verbatim source substring (verified by grounding/verify.py).

base.name: banach-fixed-point
base.text: Let (X,d) be a complete metric space and let T be a self-map on X that is a contraction: there is a constant k in [0,1) such that d(T(x),T(y)) <= k d(x,y) for all x,y in X. Then T has a unique fixed point x_star, and for any starting point x0 the iterates given by x_{n+1} = T(x_n) converge to x_star at a linear rate, with d(x_n, x_star) <= k^n d(x0, x_star).

target.name: problem_07-q1
target.text: Consider the retraining mapping T(w) = argmin over theta of L(theta; w). We prove that for any theta, theta', ||T(theta) - T(theta')|| <= (epsilon beta / gamma) ||theta - theta'||. Let kappa = epsilon beta / gamma. Since kappa < 1 and Theta is closed, T is a strict contraction. By the Banach Fixed-Point Theorem there exists a unique fixed point theta_star = T(theta_star). By the contraction property and induction, ||theta_t - theta_star|| <= kappa^t ||theta_0 - theta_star||, so the sequence converges to theta_star at a linear rate. Setting kappa^t ||theta_0 - theta_star|| <= delta and solving gives the minimum number of iterations t >= ln(||theta_0 - theta_star|| / delta) / ln(1/kappa).

groundings.iter_count: minimum number of iterations t >= ln(||theta_0 - theta_star|| / delta) / ln(1/kappa)
